fact: November 19 is exactly 323 days into the year, which means there are only 42 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is when squirrels typically go nuts burying their winter stash - they can bury up to 25 nuts per hour!
The sun usually sets before 5 PM in most northern cities around this date, making it feel like bedtime when you're still doing homework.
November 19 falls in the middle of Scorpio season for those who follow zodiac signs.
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, November 19 is often when jacaranda trees bloom with purple flowers that carpet the ground like nature's confetti.
This date sits exactly one month before the winter solstice in the north, meaning daylight is shrinking by about 2-3 minutes every single day.
Deer in cold regions are typically growing their thickest winter coats right now - their fur becomes so dense that snow sits on top without even melting!
When there's a full moon on November 19, it often appears extra bright because the air is crisp and clear in late autumn.
In northern forests, this is usually peak time for trees to drop their leaves - a single oak tree can dump 200,000 leaves in just two weeks!
November 19 marks the time when many bears in cold climates are getting seriously sleepy and looking for the perfect cave to hibernate in.
The sunrise on this date often creates amazing orange and pink colors because the sun sits low in the sky, filtering light through more atmosphere.
In many northern places, November 19 is often when the first hard frost hits, turning spider webs into glittering crystal masterpieces that look like fairy jewelry.
